did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning methods, specifically progressive transfer learning with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and adversarial domain adaptation using Cycle-GAN, to perform cross-domain skin disease classification and melanoma detection on the HAM10000 and MoleMap datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing two novel methods (progressive transfer learning and adversarial domain adaptation using Cycle-GAN) for cross-domain skin disease classification. It details specific methodologies, datasets (HAM10000, MoleMap), model architectures (ResNet-152, Cycle-GAN), and reports quantitative experimental results (accuracy, AUC, sensitivity, specificity) in tables to validate the proposed techniques, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Progressive Transfer Learning and Adversarial Domain Adaptation for Cross-Domain Skin Disease Classification
journal: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of domain shift in cross-domain skin disease classification by proposing two methods: progressive transfer learning and adversarial domain adaptation using Cycle-GAN. The authors utilized the HAM10000 and MoleMap datasets, which contain dermoscopic and normal camera images from different clinical cohorts, to evaluate generalization on melanoma and cancer detection tasks. The progressive transfer learning approach involved fine-tuning a ResNet-152 model pre-trained on ImageNet through an intermediate dataset before targeting the final domain. Additionally, Cycle-GAN was employed to translate source domain images to the target domain, creating synthetic data to improve model robustness against domain shifts. Experimental results demonstrated that both methods significantly alleviated performance drops caused by domain shift, with adversarial domain adaptation showing particularly outstanding improvements in binary classification accuracy for melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for binary classification tasks (Melanoma vs Benign, Cancer vs Non-cancer). Listed as class 17 in MoleMap (6608 samples) and class 5 in HAM10000 (1113 samples).
Included in multi-class classification and cancer detection tasks. Listed as class 19 in MoleMap (16266 samples) and class 2 in HAM10000 (514 samples).

Included in multi-class classification and cancer detection tasks. Listed as class 20 in MoleMap (2261 samples). Abbreviated as AKIEC in Table VII results (likely referring to Actinic Keratosis/Intraepithelial Carcinoma/SCC group common in HAM10000).
Grouped as a single class in HAM10000 (327 samples) and as class 22 in MoleMap (28051 samples). Abbreviated as AKIEC in Table VII.
Grouped as a single class in HAM10000 (1099 samples) and as class 24 in MoleMap (14280 samples). Abbreviated as BKL in Table VII.
Included in multi-class classification. Listed as class 14 in MoleMap (2310 samples) and class 4 in HAM10000 (115 samples). Abbreviated as DF in Table VII.

Majority class in HAM10000 (6705 samples) and includes multiple subtypes in MoleMap (Dysplastic, Compound, Regressing, Irritated, Acral, Blue, Dermal, Junctional naevus). Abbreviated as NV in Table VII.
Grouped as a single class in HAM10000 (142 samples) and as class 13 in MoleMap (855 samples). Abbreviated as VASC in Table VII.
Class 0 in MoleMap (386 samples) and Class 23 in MoleMap (3045 samples). HAM10000 does not list a distinct 'Lentigo' class separate from AKIEC or BKL in Table III, but MoleMap distinguishes them.
Class 18 in MoleMap (293 samples). Likely corresponds to melanoma subtypes.
Class 21 in MoleMap (1819 samples). Often grouped with Actinic Keratosis in HAM10000.
Class 16 in MoleMap (606 samples). Not explicitly listed in HAM10000 Table III.
Class 15 in MoleMap (382 samples). Not explicitly listed in HAM10000 Table III.
Class 9 in MoleMap (2020 samples). HAM10000 groups keratotic lesions into BKL.
Class 10 in MoleMap (208 samples). HAM10000 groups into BKL.
Class 11 in MoleMap (204 samples). HAM10000 groups into BKL.
Class 12 in MoleMap (217 samples). HAM10000 groups into AKIEC.
Used in binary classification task 'Melanoma vs Benign'. Composed of Nevus, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion, and other benign classes from the datasets.
Used in binary classification task 'Cancer vs Non-cancer'. Composed of all benign classes.
Used in binary classification task 'Cancer vs Non-cancer'. Composed of Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Bowen's disease.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (7-class skin disease classification) and binary labels (Melanoma vs. Benign, Cancer vs. Non-cancer).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'cross-domain skin disease classification'. It evaluates performance using accuracy for 7 overlapping classes (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC) and binary classification tasks for 'Melanoma vs. Benign' and 'Cancer vs. Non-cancer'. Metrics reported include Accuracy (AC), Sensitivity (SEN), Specificity (SPC), and AUC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available skin disease dataset collected from the ISIC archive, consisting of 10,015 dermoscopic images collected over 20 years from Australia and Austria. It contains 7 overlapping classes with MoleMap. Images were digitized from digital dermatoscopic images and non-digital diapositives.

use.training: Used as the target domain for dataset domain adaptation (DDA) and modality domain adaptation (MDA). 70% of images in each class were used for training.
use.testing: 30% of images in each class were randomly selected for testing.
use.other: Used as the target domain for synthesizing HAM-like images from MoleMap source domain using Cycle-GAN.

datasets_used.name: MoleMap
datasets_used.description: A clinical dataset collected from a tele-dermatology labelling standard environment, containing 102,451 images with 25 skin conditions. Each lesion has two images: a macro (clinical) image taken at 10 cm distance and a micro (dermoscopic) image. It includes 22 benign and 3 cancerous categories.

use.training: Used as the source domain for dataset domain adaptation (DDA) and modality domain adaptation (MDA). 85% of images in each category were used for training.
use.testing: 15% of images in each category were randomly partitioned for testing.
use.other: Used as the source domain for synthesizing MoleMap-like images from HAM target domain using Cycle-GAN. Also used for modality adaptation (clinical to dermoscopy).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To visualize and quantitatively analyze the intra-class and inter-class variation of the classification model's embeddings to validate the generalization capability and discrimination capacity of the domain adaptation schemes.

statistical_methods_used.method: Intra-class distance calculation (mean and standard deviation of embedding centers)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the intra-class variance and separation between different classes in the embedding space, where higher mean values indicate better separation.
To measure the variation in distances between class centers, assessing the density and separation of data points.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, AUC) and visualizations (ROC curves, t-SNE plots) without statistical significance testing.

architecture_details.augmentation: Horizontal flipping, random cropping (224x224), normalization (zero mean, unit variance)

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Initial learning rate Lr0 with decay factor Df every N_DE epochs
training_details.parameter_settings: P rS = [Lr0, Df, N DE] (specific values varied by experiment, e.g., [0.0001, 0.2, 15] for CIFAR100 experiments)
training_details.data_split: HAM: 70% train / 30% test; MoleMap: 85% train / 15% test

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully supervised deep convolutional neural network classifier
classification_or_output_head.classes: 7-class (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC) and binary tasks (Melanoma vs Benign, Cancer vs Non-cancer)

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization with zero mean and unit variance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used for Dataset Domain Adaptation (DDA) and Modality Domain Adaptation (MDA). Cycle-GAN was trained to translate images from source domain (MoleMap) to target domain (HAM) and vice versa. The number of synthesized images was controlled by percentage of the original target dataset size (10%, 20%, 50%, 100%). Training samples for Cycle-GAN varied (N=1000, 2000, 4000) with corresponding epochs (100, 200, 200). Label distributions (benign/cancer ratio) were adjusted to introduce variability.
Applied during training as a standard image augmentation technique.
Images were randomly cropped to 224x224 pixels during training.
Images were normalized with zero mean and unit variance before being fed into the network. Specifically, X = (X - Mn) / Std.
For testing data, images were resized to 256x256 and then center-cropped to 224x224.

conclusions: Simple data fusion techniques are ineffective for solving domain shift problems in cross-domain skin disease recognition.

Progressive transfer learning (fine-tuning on an intermediate dataset before the target domain) outperforms one-step transfer learning and training from scratch in terms of overall accuracy and AUC for multi-class and binary classification tasks.
Adversarial domain adaptation using Cycle-GAN to synthesize target-domain-like images from the source domain significantly alleviates performance drops caused by domain shift.

The adversarial domain adaptation method (specifically using MoleMap as source and HAM10000 as target with augmentation) achieved the best classification performance and generalization capability among the tested models.
Dataset domain adaptation improves both the model's classification performance and its generalization capability to the source domain dataset.
The proposed methods effectively address the challenge of domain shift between different clinical cohorts and imaging modalities (dermoscopy vs. normal camera) for melanoma and cancer detection.
important_remarks: The study relies on two specific datasets (HAM10000 and MoleMap) with significant class imbalance; for instance, HAM10000 has 66.95% Melanocytic Nevus and only 1.15% Dermatofibroma, which may bias model performance towards majority classes.
The HAM10000 dataset contains images from Austria that were digitized from non-digital diapositives, introducing potential quality variations compared to the digital images from Australia.

The MoleMap dataset includes tele-dermatology labeled images where many lesions and atypical nevi are 'overly conservative labelled as cancer' to avoid false negatives, introducing label noise that may affect the model's learning of benign features.

The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or statistical significance tests; performance comparisons are based on descriptive statistics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, AUC) and visualizations.
The Cycle-GAN domain adaptation method assumes high dependency between source and target domains; the authors note that the method may collapse when translating domains with large structural changes.
The study evaluates generalization using cross-sectional experiments on external datasets but does not report results from independent, external clinical cohorts outside of HAM10000 and MoleMap.

Data leakage risks are mitigated by using distinct training/test splits (70/30 for HAM, 85/15 for MoleMap) and separate source/target domains for adaptation, but the synthetic data generation process (Cycle-GAN) introduces variability based on controlled label distribution adjustments rather than natural clinical distribution.

The progressive transfer learning approach outperforms one-step transfer learning and training from scratch, suggesting that intermediate domain fine-tuning is critical for handling domain shift in this context.
